data.title: Prologis Q3 Earnings: Charging Through The Market
data.description:
Today, we circle back to one of the behemoths of real estate. Prologis, Inc. (NYSE:PLD) is the largest industrial REIT and one of the largest publicly traded real estate companies in the world. The past two years have been a bumpy ride for PLD as the company does its best to outpace tepid demand in industrial real estate.

Over the past few months, a focal point of my coverage has been the importance of adaptability within a business model while not straying too far from the core mission. All too often, companies look to create value by adding creativity to the mix. While creativity is positive, it can often lead to straying off the beaten path. Recently, I have highlighted several examples in the real estate sphere. Perhaps the best example is Brandywine Realty Trust (BDN), whose foray into life science has cost the company precious dollars.

Even still, creativity is often necessary to add value, especially for some of the largest participants in the market. Since the last time we talked about PLD, the company has done well. Let’s dive back into PLD and see what this REIT has been up to.

Review Of Prior Coverage

Over the past 12 months, I have published two articles on the industrial behemoth. First, in December, I published an article rating the company a Strong Buy. My rationale was that the industrial real estate market had reached a glut and that top-tier operators such as PLD were well positioned to capitalize. Shares of the company had declined significantly since their peaks during the post-pandemic era as the industrial fervor began to die down. Meanwhile, earnings remained solid, and PLD began to trade at the cheapest valuation in years. Below was a summary thesis where I said we should “buy the best at a bargain”:

REITs are falling left and right as demand wavers, rates remain higher for longer, and macroeconomic uncertainty clouds the next several years. Industrial has been impacted significantly more than others and the largest headliners like PLD are suffering. From my perspective, this could be an extraordinary long term buying opportunity…

Since publication, shares of PLD have performed well, appreciating by around 20% since the beginning of the year.

In a follow-up article posted several months later, I discussed PLD’s massive internal success. Specifically, I pointed towards their success in expanding into the booming data center segment. Over the past two years, the proliferation of generative AI has created generational demand for data center capacity. Specifically, we took a look at a new asset in Elk Grove, IL, to understand why these data centers have been so successful. Going further, we discussed PLD’s desire to take their power capacity up to 10 gigawatts.

At that point, the question was less about how PLD was doing internally and more about how the market was interpreting their moves to sustain growth amidst a dreary external marketplace. Let’s dive into some more recent updates that show why PLD has continued to succeed.

Q3 Earnings

On October 15th, PLD reported earnings for Q3. By and large, earnings were strong as the company posted record leasing activity as well as additional investment that secured 5.2 gigawatts of power for their data center projects. Based on this power capacity, the company has the potential to generate approximately $15 billion of new data centers. Let's dive deeper into earnings and understand the deeper success.

In the face of tariffs, sentiment and leasing velocity are improving as tenants begin to understand that the long-term approaches to their business model will likely outlast the current administration. On that theme, leasing has accelerated, and PLD posted around 62,000,000 square feet of leasing activity, causing portfolio occupancy to tick up to 95.3%. Leasing activity corresponds to a mark-to-market of 19% for the leases. Perhaps more impressively, leasing activity meant that net absorption for the quarter was 47 million square feet. The company noted that around 60 million square feet would be their normal leasing velocity on a forward basis.

Over the past year, many investors have begun to question the mark-to-market story of industrial REITs who claim that sustained rent growth will be able to elevate earnings over the long term as their internal leases roll over. Some have questioned whether the ongoing demand glut will impact this thesis and cause market rents to begin falling back to earth. Five years after the pandemic, PLD is still marking up their leases in the mid-double digits. The company also noted that same-store NOI growth was nearly 5% for the year.

From that perspective, I would consider many of those skeptics to be proven wrong. The company's chief financial officer noted that they signed nine build-to-suit agreements during the quarter, totaling 21 projects for the year. Combined with rapid data center activity, PLD is clearly firing on all cylinders trying to maintain pole position as one of the most desirable industrial real estate companies.

The company is paying for these deals with the $2.3 billion of financing activity during the quarter and their broader capital from their balance sheet. The company noted that as of quarter end, they're in a good place. The cost of debt was just 3.2%.

Looking forward, PLD maintained an accelerated rate of business activity. Their guidance for annual development starts was still around $3 billion. Meanwhile, the company's disposition and contribution guidance was raised to around $2 billion.

By all accounts, the company issued an extraordinary earnings report during the quarter. Analysts seem to have reinforced the sentiment by congratulating the company on their leadership transition plan, which includes the CEO moving to the executive position on the board of directors. Occupancy is improving, leasing is accelerating, and the company has plenty of ammunition to continue executing on their data center venture.

Risks

By most accounts, the ongoing success of PLD speaks for itself as the company's quarter-over-quarter earnings continue to improve. Nearly every metric reported by the company moved in a positive direction during the quarter, which has been noted by analysts and the market as the company's stock continues to climb over the past several days. The past week has marked a significant appreciation in the company's share price.

Amidst the success, it becomes increasingly important to also understand the risks as investor enthusiasm takes over and PLD begins to become a diamond once again.

Even as the company continues to capitalize on the extraordinary success that they've generated over the past decade, securing themselves as the predominant capital provider in industrial real estate, there are emergent risks that continue to materialize.

One sector example is increasing credit risk. The past month has begun to expose cracks in the lending sphere. One example is the bankruptcy of First Brands, which continues to send reverberations through the credit markets as lenders and landlords begin to question the credit risk and accuracy of their estimations of financial health. For PLD, who has an enormous portfolio filled with diverse tenants, they will almost certainly have some exposure to any major economic shifts. The question becomes whether the management team is able to navigate risks and protect shareholders appropriately. The company's exposure is across the globe and industries. This means that a well-diversified business model will likely protect PLD in the long term from short-term bumps. That said, a credit crisis can also have wide-reaching impacts on REITs that rely heavily on capital providers to sustain their business.

Another example of an ongoing risk is the elevated market vacancy rate in industrial real estate. According to Cushman & Wakefield, industrial vacancy sits at 7.1%, around 30 basis points above the historical average. Following the extraordinary supply that came online over the past several years, industrial REITs have been struggling to properly absorb all of the space. While demand is expected to improve going forward, also supporting rent growth, management expects vacancy to remain a little higher for longer.

Finally, there is a significant risk facing their ongoing projects in the data center segment… power. In the past, I have highlighted that power constraints would have a real impact on nearly every sector as AI becomes increasingly hungry for wattage. Faster than expected, power has already become the primary constraint for new development. The company is working to get around constraints with their energy segment, as highlighted on the Q3 earnings call.

Finally, our energy business delivered 28 megawatts of solar generation and storage in the quarter. With 825 megawatts of current capacity, we are on track to deliver on our 1-gigawatt goal by year-end. Interest from customers remains robust against the backdrop of increasing energy prices and forecasted shortages in power. We continue to integrate our solar storage and off-grid energy solutions with our real estate, another example of how Prologis continues to evolve with and for our customers.

Conclusion

While risks are always a concern, especially when the market becomes enthusiastic, PLD is actively mitigating each in a unique way. The scale and diversity of their tenancy protects against pocketed tenancy risks. Their market power has already helped them beat the market vacancy rate, still improving over the prior quarter. Finally, their energy business is expanding to support their own data center ecosystem. I see PLD as an extraordinary and unique company that offers significant value to investors of all shapes and sizes. While the company no longer offers the extraordinary value that we saw last December, the best could be yet to come. PLD earns a Buy rating.

data.title: Wall Street Roundup: Financial Earnings, Golden Highs, Data Dearth
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Big week for financial earnings (0:20). Dearth of economic data given government shutdown (3:20). AI dealmaking (6:50). CAT's valuation (10:40). Gold hitting new highs (13:30). Bitcoin and crypto liquidations (15:20). Bond update (17:15).

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: Brian Stewart, our Director of news at Seeking Alpha, we've been away for a couple weeks. Welcome back.

Brian Stewart: Great to be back.

RS: We've got trade war considerations. We've got some bank earnings. We've got a prolonged government shutdown. Where are we starting this week?

BS: I think financial stocks is the best place to start. As you point out, it was a big week for financial earnings. That's usually the leading edge of the earning season is the financial stocks release their results. So that was the theme this week.

And we actually saw a lot of love for the big financial names when they released earnings earlier this week. So Wells Fargo (WFC) was one of the biggest upside results from that. It jumped 7% in the day after its results.

Meanwhile, you saw Morgan Stanley (MS) up 5%, Citi (C) up 4%, Bank of America (BAC) up 4%. You saw strength in deal making and investment banking, which tracks with the overall anecdotal evidence that we have.

IPO market has been opening up. You see a lot of deals being announced. So I think that that fits with the general themes that we've been seeing lately.

Also on the economic front, you didn't see a huge dent in consumer credit that the big banks were announcing. So it's a sign that the consumer is holding up. Their loan delinquencies are not going up dramatically.

That was a positive view. But then later in the week, we got hit by some concerning news from series of regional banks. So you saw Zion Bancorp (ZION) dropping 13% on Thursday after a 50,000,000 loan write down.

You saw Jefferies (JEF) drop 11% after it revealed exposure to a bankrupt auto parts maker, and then Western Alliance (WAL) was down 11% after it announced it had to sue a significant borrower for alleged fraud.

You saw these losses carry over to other regional banks. So there seems to be a little bit of a split.

There seems to be an advantage in the financial world being large, diversified, tied to the Wall Street aspect of things, whereas the private credit sector is beginning to see a few concerning situations, companies failing and rolling over into the financial sector.

And then to top it all off or at least to summarize the situation, you had Jamie Dimon give kind of a now famous quote where he said, when you see one cockroach - I'm paraphrasing, when you see one cockroach, you tend to see more.

He was talking about these higher profile failures that have been cropping up lately in the private credit market. So that is a concerning sign as we roll into a time of economic uncertainty.

RS: Speaking of that economic uncertainty, what would you say about the dearth of economic data releases given the government shutdown? What would you say about or are there parallels? Are there things that we may learn once the shutdown ends?

BS: I think that's the biggest result so far of the government shutdown.

The market has been pretty resilient. The shutdown's been going on for seventeen days so far. It started in October. So basically, the entire month so far, it's been shut down.

This is now the third longest shutdown in US history. The longest one took place in Trump's first term, and then there was a a twenty one day one during the 1995/1996 time frame.

Now we're into the seventeenth day that'll almost certainly pass the twenty one days of that nineties Clinton era shutdown. There's not gonna be any movement till at least Monday. The Senate is shut down until then. So there's not even a glimmer of a hope that it would happen over the weekend.

So the next stage in terms of becoming the longest shutdown in history is another thirty five days is the one in the Trump era. So I think that's in play. It doesn't seem like there's a lot of movement in terms of deal making.

So it seems like congress and the president are happy to let this ride out, kind of just sending snipes at each other. And the market seems to have mostly shrugged it out.

But as you're pointing out, I think the dearth of data is the biggest consequence so far.

We're gonna get some CPI data next week. It was supposed to come this week, but it was delayed because of the shutdown. Also, we didn't get the last monthly jobs report because the shutdown. So in a lot of ways, investors are flying blind on that front.

I think the inflation data will be interesting. I don't know how much it'll change the outlook unless it's a dramatic either spike or disinflationary, data. I think most people kind of figure it'll be in line with what we've been seeing, in the 3% range, 2.8, 2.9, 3% range in terms of annual inflation rate.

So I think we're pricing in that to continue. I think the lack of jobs data is a bigger deal just because that was showing signs of weakening as we went into it. We had not only soft jobs growth in the months that are being announced, but there were a lot of revisions in the data, famously, leading to the head of the statistical agency getting fired by Trump.

So we could come out of it like you're pointing out. We could come out of it with jobs data where we catch up for a few months and find out that things have been bad for a while. Or I suppose we could come out of it and find out that things have jumped back.

Like I like I said, the data coming from things like earnings reports and other economic indicators, the private sector economic indicators have been pretty mixed.

You see positive signs. And so I think the market generally is cautiously optimistic. And in terms of the the stock market letting the AI enthusiasm churn the market higher, broadly speaking.

RS: Speaking of that AI enthusiasm, what would you say investors are the most enthusiastic about these days and any highlights to point out?

BS: I still think we're living in a situation where the deal making economy is what's driving the market.

You've seen a lot of situations where stocks get a lot of love after announcing a big deal with a major AI player or you have, like in the case like Intel (INTC), you have a deal where they are receiving investment from a company like Nvidia (NVDA).

And so that theme has continued. So for instance, in the past week, we saw OpenAI announce three different deals. They announced a deal with Broadcom (AVGO). They announced a deal with Salesforce (CRM). And and a really interesting one with Walmart (WMT).

Walmart ticked up 5% on Tuesday and reached a 52 week high after announcing that it partnered with OpenAI to use ChatGPT to build out an enhanced AI fueled shopping and checkout system.

This also plugs into a theme you and I have been talking about for a while where we're seeing the spread of - I'm trying to think of a positive way to say this - but the AI contagion, whatever the positive way to frame that would be, the way it's been spreading out to from the very AI centric stocks, the ones involved in the infrastructure build out and things like that.

And now it's moving into other parts of the economy.

So you see Walmart starting to get a significant pop. 5% is nothing to sneeze at for a company the size of Walmart. You see you get a significant pop for just integrating AI into it.

And so you can view that two ways. The people who are arguing that we're in an AI bubble could point back to the dotcom era when companies would add dotcom to the name just to get a pop in the stock price.

So I think the bears would would argue this is just a sign more sign of froth where companies are getting significant movements in their market cap from what are relatively small tweaks of their business.

But the bulls could then argue that you're seeing the AI economy spread out of the collection of the central five to 10 stocks that we've all been talking about for the last couple years and seeing it touch the broader economy and more stocks.

So if you're a bull, you're seeing this concentration the market has seen, you're seeing this spread out a little bit.

I think on that front, it's worth checking on a stock that we talked about a month ago, Caterpillar (CAT).

So when we talked about a month ago, Caterpillar was up 28% year to date. It had gotten a boost as a stock that would benefit from the physical build out of the AI infrastructure.

So actually building the data centers. Caterpillar provides the heavy machinery for those kind of building products. And we've seen a lot of deals related to those AI build out.

I mean, one of the high profile ones was NVIDIA and OpenAI getting together to build out OpenAI's AI infrastructure. And so in the past month, you've seen Caterpillar rise another 20%, so it's now up 48 year to date.

So it's a good example of of a stock that you wouldn't immediately jump to mind as an AI stock, but it's been getting sustained benefit from this AI build out over the past year to date so far, but even in the last month, so dramatically.

RS: It's interesting. Something we've been talking about here and also on the Investing Experts Podcast is this overlapping nature of sectors these days. Tech is everywhere. You know, everything is overlapping.

And to your point, even if the companies aren't necessarily overlapping in their focus and their sector, but they can have different things can affect them, like the economy and the tariffs can affect Caterpillar in a certain way that might not have been true a few years ago.

It's interesting to see markets evolve and develop, and investors have to take note of these new developments, I would say.

BS: I think underlying that with Caterpillar is interesting too because Caterpillar's valuation obviously has changed dramatically as the stock has risen and especially for the type of stock it is.

It's being treated as a tech company, but it's this old line heavy machinery company, it creates a debate within the investor.

And you can see it in our investors as well. If you go to look at some of the recent analysis that we've published on Caterpillar, you see some sells in there based on valuation just based on the idea that this has gone too far.

And Caterpillar is still sensitive to tariffs. It's still sensitive to a cyclical economy. Like I said, there's kind of mixed to cautious economic indicators recently, and a company like Caterpillar traditionally sees a downturn during times like that.

RS: A lot to keep in mind for sure as as we watch these things unfold. Also, that notion of valuation also has been a big theme that we've been discussing this year. Certainly, still being discussed. It seems like these valuation concerns are going to continue for the time being.

I also have a question as we're talking about AI and tech, and I mentioned at the start these trade war concerns with China. How is that playing into things? What would you say about tech players in China, outside of China? How are you assessing that?

BS: So the broader market dipped last Friday as the words between the US and China kinda hit a peak, but has largely ignored it since. The markets are are basically flat this week.

I think that from the April time frame when the stocks fell dramatically in the initial rollout of the tariffs and then rebounded relatively quickly as Trump softens the tariff message. There was a series of delays and then negotiations and things like that.

I think there's a sense that this is all part of the process, that the sniping back and forth between China and the US and whatever other hotspots in the trade landscape that might pop up.

I think there's a feeling that it'll eventually settle into an acceptable conclusion. I think that's what's happening here.

And you already saw Trump kind of walk back some of the statements. He said something like 100% tariffs on China is unsustainable.

So suggesting that even if it went into place, this would be a temporary situation. So it feels like leverage. It feels like part of the deal making process, and I think that's how the markets are treating it.

RS: And what would you say about gold (XAUUSD:CUR)? It's hitting record highs. It feels like a natural part of all this economic conversation we've been having. It feels like a natural result of that. What would you say about gold right now or gold and silver if you'd care to talk about precious metals in general?

BS: I think gold is is interesting just kind of the dichotomy of the things that have been performing well this year are those AI stocks that we've been talking about and gold.

So you have kind of the new new thing, in AI related stuff, but you also have basically the world's oldest market in gold hitting.

RS: That's such an interesting take. I have not heard that. That's interesting.

BS: The movement in gold is is eye popping, you know? It's hard to fathom.

So gold is up 62% year to date. It peaked recently just below $4,380 an ounce. A lot of it's being there's inflation concerns built into it. There's a rush to safety amid economic uncertainty.

So you have this small pocket of the stock market that's been doing well in the AI stocks. Then you have a mixed result through the rest of the market, and then you'd have money for gold. So if you just take the stock market as a single investment portfolio and there seems to be sort of a barbell philosophy going on where we have what's traditionally seen as the safest investment, a place to to just park your money and wait out rough economic times.

And then you have these bets on these high growth stocks going on. And then in the middle of the market, there's a stock picker situation.

RS: In terms of crypto, we've also seen these liquidations this morning and broader market sell offs. Anything to say about the crypto market and Bitcoin (BTC-USD) specifically?

BS: I think, comparing it to gold is instructive. The promise of Bitcoin always related to its inflation hedge that here's something that the central banks can't fiddle with.

There's a certain amount of of Bitcoin, and there's only gonna be a certain amount of Bitcoin. So that's sort of part of the underlying investment argument for Bitcoin. But since its inception, Bitcoin has not traded as an inflation hedge, just traded more like a tech stock.

And we see that playing out in the most recent trading as well as gold has continued to push forward and reach continual new highs, recently, Bitcoin has been much more volatile.

Bitcoin reached $125,000. I think it peaked a little bit above to $126,000. But now it's back to $106,000. So you're seeing these more dramatic swings in Bitcoin. I think it's a sign that the crypto market still isn't a fully mature market.

I think you're seeing a lot of trading going on. I think there's a lot of market related especially if things start to get tough and you need cash to run your business and you've had some money parked in crypto, I mean, crypto might be the first place that you liquidate to raise cash for your business.

And similarly, if you have an investment portfolio, maybe your first stop in terms of raising cash is to go to that crypto. And I'm just speculating now and why there might be increased liquidations there.

But it's possible that's become for some people the first place they go to when it comes to cash.

RS: Also, if you would give a brief update on the bond market, what we're looking at there.

BS: Using the ten year as sort of a proxy for the market in general, have been declining recently.

So if we look back into to June, we were at the the 4.5 level in late May, June area, now we're down to the 4% mark in bonds.

You do see this buying in the bond market, which I think dovetails with the situation in the gold market where you see this flight to safety, as we look to the worrisome economic situation.

It's hard to characterize the economic situation because I feel like there's a lot of fear that there's an AI bubble, that the smallest little bit could could trigger a decline there.

Meanwhile, the stock market has been pretty resilient, even as those worries have come up.

Like we said before, we're flying blind in terms of the economic data, at least over the last couple of weeks, just because of the government shutdown.

There have been signs of weakening, but there's also continuing signs of resilience there too. So I think traders just in general are having trouble characterizing the situation.

I just there's a lot of balls in the air. There's arrows pointing in multiple directions. And so as we talk about with the financial stocks, the general mood coming out of those large bank earnings was pretty solid.

The stocks did pretty well after earnings were announced. The commentary of around them was pretty solid, at least cautiously optimistic.

But then we had in the last couple days, we've had this worry about the regional bank sector. And so there's just sort of this idea that maybe there's something lurking in the corners.

There's something that we're not pricing in that's going to jump out. I mean, this is a good Halloween metaphor, I think.

And the bond market might be a great place to keep an eye on for those kind of fears, because as we've talked about before, there's sort of the reputation on Wall Street that if you want to take in the general mood about the economy, the general mood about the overall situation, the bond market is usually the place that has that priced in, most directly.

RS: I appreciate the Halloween metaphor. Definitely a lot of fear and excitement depending on your perspective, depending on where you are looking, depending on your history and experience, and all of those things matter and play a part into how much fear I bet you're feeling right now as an investor.

Curious what we have to look forward to next week?

BS: So earning season continues. Tesla (TSLA) obviously is going to be the biggest name reporting next week. \

I feel like we haven't talked about Tesla a little bit. I mean, part of that is we we haven't talked in a couple weeks, but I feel like it's kind of pulled back from the spotlight a little bit since Elon Musk went back to his full time job.

I feel like Tesla's been a little bit off the radar, with the the focus recently being more on the nexus of AI deal making surrounded NVIDIA and OpenAI and some of the other companies making noise there.

And so it'll be interesting to get a check-in with Tesla, to sort of see how that's going.

And then some other names next week, Netflix (NFLX) is coming out. So that could be an interesting consumer name. Just, how are people spending their money?

We're also gonna get a couple of big automakers. (GM) and Ford (F) are both announcing. So along with Tesla, we're gonna get a pretty good idea of the automaking sector in general and EVs in particular.

And then, Texas Instruments (TXN) and Intel on the earnings front. And Amazon (AMZN), obviously, is going to be another giant name along with Tesla to look at.

RS: Texas Instruments and Intel feel like the gold/AI conversations, like what's old and new.

BS: Yeah. I mean, Intel's gonna be an interesting one just because of the wave of investment that it's received. It received money from the US government and then it received money in the deal with NVIDIA.

So it'll just be interesting to hear the commentary coming out of those deals where Intel leadership sees where the company is and what the plans are going forward.

And then, Amazon is always interesting in terms of the commentary because you obviously have the retailing giant part of it, which is plugged in, central to the economy in a way that a lot of companies aren't.

So I think we're getting a lot of information, economic information from Amazon, which is useful in a situation where we're not getting all the government information we usually do.

But also Amazon with the AWS segment, that's plugged in, central to technology broadly. So seeing how that business is doing is also gonna be very interesting, just for the segment that we've been talking about, that leading edge tech segment.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Nvidia: It's Too Profitable, I'll Pass
data.description:
Thesis

Believe it or not, high profit margins can make me nervous. Basic economic theory states that profits will attract competition that then drives prices downward and eventually consumes the profit margin. Real-world economics is more complex than chalkboard economics, and often companies can sustain healthy profit margins and use moats to resist competitors. That said, I believe immensely high profit margins can be serious risks as there’s more incentive for challengers to try to pierce moats, and moats can also encourage stakeholders to work together to present a united front.

With the above in mind, I’ve concluded that Nvidia is simply too profitable for its own good. It’s not just about competitors, either. We’re also seeing emerging efforts by customers to diversify away from sole reliance on Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA). I believe Nvidia’s customers are looking at Nvidia’s margins with envy, and perhaps asking if they should be paying such a large markup. For now, many simply have to pay up, but I believe NVDA's moat will one day fall. Of course, NVDA will likely remain a tech goliath, but its profit margins and thus market cap may be forced back down to earth.

NVDA: A Moat Story

I’ve been wondering about Nvidia’s moat and whether or not it is sustainable. I’ve scoured Reddit threads, Seeking Alpha articles, and various other places to see what other people think, and opinions are mixed. So is Nvidia’s moat nigh impenetrable? I can’t say for certain, but I do believe defending it will be far more difficult than many NVDA bears believe.

Let’s discuss what NVDA’s moat is, and we’ll start by defining what it is not: the moat is less about the GPUs themselves, and rather about the CUDA programming model, as well as other tools and services. Essentially, it’s about the ecosystem as a whole, and I think Nvidia’s impressive GPUs are actually one of the less important aspects of the moat.

Competitors like Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) can already build high-performance hardware but so far no one has built a CUDA-breaking programming model, and the overall ecosystem is rather weak. Further, many companies are already deep in the NVDA ecosystem, and decoupling or diversifying would be a large undertaking. Still, efforts are emerging, like AMD’s open-source Radeon Open Compute platform ROCm, an answer to CUDA, but one still in its infancy and rather primitive. (I am emphasizing CUDA but keep in mind it’s really more the entire ecosystem.)

Diversification: A New Hope

Despite the challenges of shifting away from NVDA, companies are undertaking efforts. OpenAI (OPENAI), perhaps the biggest brand in the AI space, likely has more than a million NVDA chips but plans to grow that to 100 million. Yet OpenAI also announced a major partnership with AMD to purchase 6 gigawatts of AMD’s chips. The deal also allows OpenAI to potentially take a stake of up to 10% of AMD via warrants. OpenAI likewise struck a deal with 10 gigawatt deal with Broadcom (AVGO), which makes customized chips. This deal could be worth up to $350 billion.

These deals come after OpenAI reached an agreement with NVDA to purchase up to 10 gigawatts of NVDA’s chips. So why is OpenAI both investing in and diversifying away from NVDA? It’s all about the profits, and the fact that there’s simply too much profit, in my opinion.

Hardware often has relatively low margins, but NVDA enjoys a 52.41% TTM net income margin and 69.85% TTM gross margin. This is well above the respective sector medians of 4.51% and 49.92%. AMD, meanwhile, has a net income margin of just 9.57%, although the gross margin is quite high at 50.99%.

OpenAI likely has at least two angles in its efforts to diversify. First, diversification could be achieved, and thus OpenAI has far more leverage in negotiations with NVDA. Second, NVDA may feel pressure and want to head off competition, so it might prove willing to reduce prices and thus net income margins.

Elon Musk's xAI also works with AMD. Oracle recently purchased 50,000 AMD chips. Then there’s China, which has actually restricted Chinese companies from using NVDA chips. China is also pushing MUSA, an alternative to CUDA, and various other alternatives to NVDA and American technologies. Increasingly, it seems AI stakeholders are looking to put pressure on NVDA’s margins and to also develop viable alternatives. Meanwhile, custom chip makers, like Broadcom, can offer chips better suited for the needs of specific clients.

I think there’s a fair chance that these custom chips could be very important in the future, but for now generalist solutions, like those offered by NVDA, will probably remain the mainstay until AI technology matures.

A Clash of Giants

Finally, I want to briefly mention that other tech giants are working on custom chips, data center buildout, and various other ambitions that could erode NVDA’s moat. Amazon (AMZN) has its Trainium for model training and Inferentia chips. Reportedly, AMZN’s chips are selling at a 25% discount in comparison to NVDA chips. Alphabet (GOOGL) has Tensor and is building out vast data centers. So far, Google doesn’t seem intent on selling Tensor chips directly to people, but rather selling compute from its own servers.

Will Amazon and Alphabet succeed in mounting a strong challenge to NVDA’s market dominance? Who knows. Yet that they are pouring so many resources into trying could quickly put pressure on NVDA to sell its cheap products more cheaply.

That said, Nvidia isn’t taking any of this setting down and appears to be moving to mitigate risks by increasing investments into AI developers. The company has recently announced a rash of investments, including investments in OpenAI that could be worth $100 billion or more. This could help keep OpenAI mostly anchored in NVDA’s ecosystem. Other investments include CoreWeave (CRWV), Recursion Pharmaceuticals (RXRX), and WeRide (WRD). These investments could pressure customers into sticking with NVDA.

NVDA Fundamentals And Valuation

To be honest, I’m not sure how important fundamental and valuation analysis is for Nvidia. This is more about what story ends up being told. Does AI prove as disruptive as foretold? Does compute demand continue to scale? If yes to these questions, can NVDA sustain its moat? If the answer to this question is yes, then NVDA will likely continue to trend upward, thus invalidating my thesis and proving the bulls right.

Still, there are some fundamental and valuation considerations worth fleshing out. I’ll also provide metrics for AMD to provide content. We can start by looking at Seeking Alpha’s Quant grades. The quant tools give AMD a Strong Buy while NVDA is a Hold, with its valuation getting an F. Nvidia’s GAAP P/E TTM is a rather pricey 51.79, but is far below AMD’s 140.46 (Seeking Alpha data). However, NVDA’s EBITDA Margin is much better at 59.49% to AMD’s 18.61%.

AMD’s YOY revenue growth is a healthy 27.17%, but NVDA enjoys a blistering 71.55% growth rate. AMD’s Levered Cash Flow, after years of being stuck in neutral, has started to improve, as shown below. NVDA’s cash flow, also shown below, has been ramping up very rapidly, helping to explain the +$4 trillion market cap.

AMD Cash Flows

NVDA Cashflows

I’ve mentioned profit margins at length now, but I do think it’s crucial to point out that NVDA’s profit margin before the AI craze was still high, but also nowhere near as high as it is now. I believe that NVDA will eventually regress back towards this mean, and that will make its market cap hard to sustain.

What Could Prove Me Wrong?

I already mentioned NVDA maintaining its moat. If the company does, my thesis will likely be disproven. That’s not the only way NVDA can prove me wrong, however. The company’s investments in various companies could bear fruit and help sustain NVDA’s massive market cap. For all we know, NVDA might end up with a 10% stake in the next Nvidia and another 10% in the next Microsoft, Uber, or whatever else.

Key Takeaway: NVDA Is Too Profitable For Its Own Good

Nvidia is one of the greatest success stories in history, and its recent growth story is worthy of the Nobel Prize in Literature (I joke). I also believe NVDA will go down in history as a monumental influence on society. At the same time, I do think that diversification and efforts to breach NVDA’s moat will force the company’s margins back down to earth and may eventually bite into Nvidia's market share.

data.title: XDTE: Increasing Risk During Volatility, Consider SPYI Instead
data.description:
Oftentimes, comparison forces investors to focus on some of the seemingly smaller or more nuanced differences that can still matter significantly over a longer period of time. While obviously analyzing an investment in a vacuum has value, a comparative framework often better highlights the advantages and disadvantages of the ETF.

Covered Call funds have been around for some time, but these options-based investments have evolved significantly in the last decade, with ETFs now offering much more sophisticated choices to investors that are often better able to achieve more of a balance between both solid income and consistent total returns as well.

Two of the more successful recently developed covered call funds are the Roundhill S&P 500 0DTE Covered Call Strategy ETF (BATS:XDTE) and the NEOS S&P 500® High Income ETF (SPYI).

The NEOS S&P 500® High Income ETF has offered investors total returns of 53.96 percent since the fund's inception in August of 2022, while the S&P 500 (SPY) composite that this investment tracks has offered investors total returns of 74.03 percent during the same time period.

The Roundhill S&P 500 0DTE Covered Call Strategy ETF has offered investors total returns of 25.2 percent since the fund's inception in March of 2024, while the S&P 500 index has offered investors total returns of 31.43 percent.

SPYI has slightly outperformed XDTE since this Roundhill fund's inception by nearly 1 percent.

I last wrote about the Roundhill S&P 500 0DTE Covered Call Strategy ETF in May of 2025, and I rated the fund a sell. I last wrote about the NEOS S&P 500® High Income ETF in September of this year, and I rated this fund a strong buy. Today, I am downgrading XDTE to a strong sell and reiterating my strong buy rating on SPYI. I wanted to update my coverage of XDTE since the financial and political environment has changed over the last several months, as the economic data has deteriorated over the last 3 months, and political tensions between the US and China have been rising. While SPYI and XDTE have both performed well in a rising market, SPYI should outperform in a range-bound or down market, and these scenarios are more likely now with new developments seen in the last several months.

The Roundhill S&P 500 0DTE Covered Call Strategy ETF has an expense ratio of .97 percent, a trailing yield of 28.51 percent, and $399.68 million in assets under management. The fund tracks the S&P 500 and has a long position in the index through call options, XDTE does not own actual equities. The options strategy used by this ETF is to maintain a long position overnight with deep in-the-money calls with no hedges and then, during the day, sell at-the-money calls that are 0 days to expiration, usually and generally .15-.19 percent out-of-the-money. The investment's options strategy is based on the empirical data showing that most of the S&P 500 gains have come during hours when US markets are closed. XDTE seeks to generate .1 percent of income a day.

The NEOS S&P 500® High Income ETF has an expense ratio of .68 percent, a trailing yield of 11.76 percent, and $5.70 billion in assets under management. SPYI owns actual stock and the fund tracks the S&P 500 closely. SPYI's strategy is to sell indexed call options that are nearly 1-3 percent, and the fund also sometimes buys out-of-the-money calls with the income received from the contracts sold. SPYI uses a data-based approach, and the fund does not always sell options against all the investment's holdings. The ETF has generally sought to distribute around 1 percent of the net asset value a month since the ETF's inception in August 2022. SPYI generally seeks to cover nearly 80 percent of the portfolio with written index calls.

Even though both SPYI and XDTE have offered investors similar returns since the Roundhill fund's inception in March of 2024, this has been in a generally more favorable market in which the S&P 500 has risen nearly 31 percent. The economic data has deteriorated over the last several months, with the August jobs report showing the US economy added the fewest number of new jobs since 2010. China has also escalated trade tensions with the US by imposing new restrictions on rare earth minerals. Both Fed Chair Powell and JP Morgan (JPM) CEO Jamie Dimon have also recently warned that equities are likely overvalued, and inflation-adjusted S&P 500 multiples remain above historical averages, with forward multiples currently around 23x projected future earnings, above the past average of 20x forecasted future earnings.

The market is more likely to be rangebound in the current economic and financial environment.

Even though the Roundhill S&P 500 0DTE Covered Call Strategy ETF leaves the fund's portfolio uncovered overnight when markets are closed, this investment has not outperformed the NEOS S&P 500® High Income ETF since XDTE's inception in March of 2024. SPYI's more nuanced and flexible approach that selectively buys out-of-the-money calls and adjusts both strike prices that monthly index calls are sold at and how much of the fund's portfolio is covered should lead this Neos investment to significantly outperform the ETF's Roundhill peer in a sideways or down market. XDTE's approach of covering the entire portfolio during the day with at-the-money call options also makes this investment higher risk if volatility levels rise, as is likely during a potential sell-off.

SPYI outperformed XDTE slightly by nearly 1 percent in less than a month during the March sell-off in 2025, and the more nuanced and flexible options strategy that this Neos fund uses should enable the more actively managed investment to navigate a more volatile market better as well. SPYI adjusts both the percentage of the fund covered by calls as well as the strike price of index calls the ETF both buys and sells, while XDTE is simply selling at-the-money index calls against the entire investment's portfolio when the market is open during the day.

SPYI also performed very well during the volatile period in the market between late July and late October of 2023, actually outperforming the S&P 500 during this period of time.

All investment theses have risks, and there is not a significant amount of data for XDTE or SPYI. Both funds have also been able to operate in what generally have been more favorable market conditions as well. Still, the options approach that this Neos fund takes should continue to enable the ETF to outperform XDTE in a range-bound or down market. Investors are also putting more trust in the managers of SPYI, since the ETF is more actively managed than XDTE, but the Neos managers have produced solid results across most of the company's funds for several years now.

There have been a number of new and much more sophisticated covered call funds that are available for investors that have less than 5 years of data available, and most of these ETFs have not truly been tested during an extended sell-off where equities are more volatile for more than a couple of months. Still, the more flexible and nuanced strategies used by SPYI and the managers' ability to navigate in a volatile market should make this ETF a better choice for most income investors than XDTE in what likely will be a weak-performing market moving forward.

data.title: ASML: Expecting A Transitional Year In 2026 (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Why ASML Can Take a Breather

Since my Buy rating upgrade exactly one year ago, ASML (NASDAQ:ASML) has delivered a total return of 43%, driven by strong earnings results in 1H amid robust AI demand. However, the Q3 FY2025 results were mixed, with revenue below the estimates. While the stock's 35x non-GAAP fwd P/E appears reasonable in today's lofty market, I see limited growth catalysts in the near term following a strong EUV upgrade cycle in 1H.

When a sector is hot, you just buy the sector leader at a fair price. Recently, we've seen several mega deals with OpenAI, including partnerships with Nvidia (NVDA) and AMD (AMD). These two leading chip designers plan to deploy at least 16 GW of new datacenter capacity over the next few years, powered by their most advanced GPU series.

I recently wrote an article reaffirming my bullish rating on TSMC (TSM), the world's leading chip manufacturer, which will produce NVDA and AMD's most advanced chips on its N2P and N2 nodes in 2027. ASML, the only global provider of ultraviolet (EUV) lithography systems, designs and builds the critical equipment required for these advanced nodes. Since TSM is ASML's largest customer, I expect ASML's long-term growth outlook to remain strong as long as AI demand does not fade.

However, the next high-NA EUV upgrade cycle could be two to three years away. With ongoing trade tensions and a growth normalization from China in FY2026. I prefer to wait for its FY2026 guidance announcement during the Q4 FY2025 earnings release. Therefore, given the +40% rally in less than two months, I'm downgrading ASML from Buy to Hold for now.

Low Growth in 2H FY2025 Is Fully Priced In

ASML delivered a mixed Q3 earnings, missing revenue estimates with only 0.7% YoY growth, even though the stock saw a modest gain in the next trading day. The midpoint of Q4 revenue guidance implies 0.1% YoY revenue decline, suggesting that the flat growth in 2H FY2025 is already priced in. Management reiterated its 15% YoY growth in net sales for FY2025 previously provided in Q2.

Net bookings beat estimates, jumping 105% YoY, but that strong number came off a low base in Q3 FY2024. This is encouraging because the demand remains strong. But keep in mind, bookings will not immediately translate into revenue. EUV bookings account for 66.7% of net bookings in Q3, up from 53.8% a year ago. The flat growth expected in 2H largely reflects a pulled-forward effect from the low-NA EUV (NXE 3800E) upgrade cycle, which has completed in 1H.

Trade Tensions vs. Robust AI Demand

Although ASML may see muted revenue growth in the near term, most analysts remain bullish on ASML's bookings growth, given its massive long-term growth opportunity from the AI chip manufacturing process. Rising demand for NVDA and AMD's advanced GPUs requires advanced nodes to produce them, which ultimately benefits ASML. However, the EUV upgrade cycle doesn't move in a straight line, and growth can be uneven from one year to another.

Despite strong AI demand, persistent U.S and China trade tensions could disrupt global semiconductor supply chains. Last week, the U.S House committee accused ASML of boosting sales to China, triggering a pullback. Moreover, China's rare earths control can significantly impact ASML's business in the long run, as they could raise costs across the supply chains. During the Q3 FY2025 earnings call, the CFO said that the company is "well prepared" with enough inventory for now, but he warned that the rare earth export control could have meaningful impact over the next few years if tensions persist.

China Growth Normalization

ASML still maintains a large revenue exposure to China. According to a recent report, China accounted for 42% of its net system sales in Q3, up from 27% in Q2. However, management noted that this year's performance reflected above-trend growth in China, and they expected a growth normalization in FY2026. That said, the company warned that China's UV revenue is expected to decline significantly next year, adding further pressure to growth on top of ongoing geopolitical tensions between the U.S and China.

FY2026 Is a Transitional Year

As mentioned earlier, ASML will provide FY2026 guidance in the next earnings release, but I think the outlook is likely muted. The company only signals that the net sales for FY2026 should not fall below FY2025 levels, which seems too cautious. Management sees EUV revenue growing next year, offset by weaker China revenue. So, the company's FY2026 revenue guidance would likely be in the low-single digit range.

Although management mentioned that the next major orders for High-NA could be in 2H FY2026, the High-NA EUV is still in its early stage and may not have massive productions until 2027. Therefore, I don't see a major revenue catalyst until that time. However, the worst-case scenario is that if trade tensions escalate further and persist into next year, ASML could face a revenue decline in FY2026,

At least, AI infrastructure spending shows no sign of slowing, which is positive enough to keep the stock near the current levels from a sentiment perspective. Over the long-term, management reiterated its 2030 revenue target of EUR 44 billion to 60 billion and gross margin of 56% to 60%. This implies a 9.8% CAGR in total revenue from 2025 to 2030 with a strong expansion in gross margin from the current 52%.

Why the Stock Looks Fairly Valued

Given the rally of more than 40% since September, ASML now trades at 35.8x non-GAAP fwd P/E, which is reasonable. However, given a flat growth in 2H FY2025 and muted FY2026 outlook, near-term upside appears limited.

Most of the low-NA EUV upgrades have already been done in 1H, and meaningful high-NA EUV productions likely won't happen until 2027, consistent with TSM's N2 volume ramp timeline. In the meantime, early high-NA EUV shipments could pressure gross margin. If macro risk materializes next year, ASML's forward valuation multiple would jump higher. That said, the stock could face more downside risk before climbing back higher. For active investors, it may be prudent to take some chips off the table now.

Conclusion

In summary, a stock's upside momentum ultimately depends on its growth outlook. Despite strong bookings, ASML's revenue growth is expected to slow in the near term, not because of weakening AI demand, but due to the pull-forward demand associated with the prior EUV upgrade cycle, causing a flat revenue growth in 2H FY2025. Looking ahead to FY2026, the company could face a significant revenue decline in China due to growth normalization. While bullish sentiment around AI may keep the stock elevated, I don't see immediate catalysts to justify a multiple expansion. Instead, ongoing trade tensions could weigh on the stock's sentiment as the company still has significant revenue exposure to China. Therefore, I'm downgrading ASML from Buy to Hold for now

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.description:
QMSK Technology Is Growing, But Margins Are Dropping

QMSK Technology Co., Ltd. (QMSK) has filed to raise $31.25 million in an IPO of its ordinary shares, according to SEC F-1/A registration information.

QMSK provides various business services to automobile insurance companies via third parties in China.

The firm is growing top line revenue, but at a cost to margins.

Given its many risks, low gross margin and overall margin compression in a fragmented industry with low barriers to entry, and expensive valuation, my outlook on the QMSK IPO is to sell (avoid it).

What Does QMSK Do?

Qingdao, China-based QMSK Technology Co., Ltd was founded to provide auto insurance risk assessment and value-added services to major insurance carriers in Mainland China.

Management is led by Chairman and CEO Mr. Yaxin Dong, who was previously the founder of several companies and has more than 13 years of insurance industry experience in various roles.

The company's primary offerings include:

  • Insurance Risk Assessment

  • Vehicle Safety Inspections

  • Vehicle Maintenance Services

  • IT Services

  • Promotional Services.

The firm provides its services to both enterprise and insurance company third-party customers.

The pie charts below show the revenue contribution percentage by segment and customer type:

QMSK has booked fair market value investment of $3.5 million as of March 31, 2025 from investors, including Yaxin Dong, Jing Shen and others.

Selling and Marketing expenses as a percentage of total revenue have risen as revenues have increased, though they remain small, as the figures below indicate:

The Selling and Marketing efficiency multiple, i.e., how many dollars of additional revenue are produced from each dollar of Selling and Marketing expense, was 72.1x in the most recent reporting period. (Source - SEC.)

What Is QMSK’s Market?

According to a 2025 market research report by Mordor Intelligence, the Chinese market for automobile insurance in 2025 is estimated to be approximately $140 billion and is forecasted to exceed $171 billion by 2030.

This represents a projected CAGR of 4.1% from 2025 to 2030.

The primary factors driving this expected growth are a surge in the use of new energy vehicles [EVs] and increasing digital distribution of insurance coverage and processing.

Also, the deregulation of online automobile policy procurement in 2020 has increased efficiency and convenience for companies and policyholders alike.

The company's services are a small part of the overall market for auto insurance.

Regionally within China, East China continues to have the highest premium volume, but Northwest China is showing an increasing growth rate, expected to be around 10%.

The automobile insurance third-party services market in China is highly fragmented, with low barriers to entry and thousands of market participants.

The competitive landscape in China’s auto insurance third-party services aftermarket consists of online aggregators and agency networks, claims & assistance outsourcing, repair network management, telematics and OEM-embedded insurance capabilities.

The company seeks clients for its services among commercial enterprises and insurance companies.

QMSK outsources most of its services to external vendors and partners who work with auto dealerships, repair and maintenance shops and 4S stores.

Its service network currently comprises over 10,650 locations in 30 province-level municipalities in Mainland China.

Below is a thumbnail SWOT analysis for QMSK:

Strengths

  • Proprietary service platform, large service network across many provinces, established partnerships

Weaknesses

  • High dependence on large enterprise customers, strict regulatory and compliance environment, reliance on outsourcing reduces control and possibly quality

Opportunities

  • Growing Chinese auto insurance market, higher demand for third-party auto insurance services, potential to go direct to insurance companies

Threats

  • Regulatory risks, intense competition in fragmented markets

QMSK’s Recent Financial Results

The firm’s recent financial performance is summarized as shown below:
