QMSK Is Growing, But Margins Are Falling

QMSK is seeking U.S. public capital market investment to fund its general corporate growth plans.

The company’s financials have generated growing topline revenue, increased gross profit but reduced (and low) gross margin, lower operating profit and operating margin but a swing to cash flow from operations.

Free cash flow for the twelve months ended March 31, 2025, was $1.8 million.

Selling and Marketing expenses as a percentage of total revenue rose to a still-low 0.4x and its Selling and Marketing efficiency multiple was a very high 72.1x in the most recent reporting period.

The firm currently plans to pay no dividends and will be subject to various capital restrictions within China and through its Cayman Islands shell, which will be the U.S. public company entity.

QMSK’s recent capital spending history shows it has spent very little on capital expenditures as a percentage of its operating cash flow.

The firm’s growth strategy includes providing customized services to clients to enhance value, expanding its marketing efforts to new enterprise clients and in underserved regions of China, and continued investment in talent development and its technology platform.

The market opportunity for providing third-party auto insurance services is large and will likely grow in proportion to the overall auto insurance industry’s growth trajectory.

Risks to the company’s outlook as a public firm include its "foreign private issuer" and "emerging growth company" status, which enables management to produce less information for shareholders.

The company has high customer concentration risks, relying on a small number of large enterprise customers for a large portion of its revenue.

It also has significant vendor concentration risks, where its services are outsourced to a limited number of third-party platforms and repair vendors.

As to valuation, QMSK is proposing to value itself at a far higher EV/EBITDA multiple and Price/Sales multiple than a U.S. industry partial peer, although QMSK is growing revenue at a fast rate and is generating earnings.

QMSK’s proposed P/E ratio is also more than 2.5 times that of the U.S. peer, despite dropping operating and net results.

Given QMSK’s pricey valuation, low gross margin, decreasing operating and net margins, fragmented industry with low barriers to entry, material operating and regulatory risks, customer and vendor concentration risks, my outlook on the IPO is to sell (avoid it).

Expected IPO Pricing Date: To be announced.

data.title: An Excellent 5-Fund Dividend Portfolio I'd Build To Retire On
data.description:
When looking to build a portfolio to retire on dividends, the biggest factors to prioritize are dividend sustainability, sufficient yield to comfortably meet living expenses, and steady dividend growth to offset inflation’s erosive effects. If you can check all three of those boxes, you can sleep well at night knowing that your dividend income will continue to flow and rise over time, allowing you to stay calm during market volatility and simply let the dividends roll in.

In this article, I will share a five-fund portfolio designed to meet all of these criteria by providing ample diversification, a high aggregate dividend yield, and sufficient dividend growth to beat inflation over time. The core of this portfolio focuses on broad sector diversification and blue-chip dividend growth machines that can deliver dependable, long-term dividend growth while maintaining a solid yield.

SCHD: The Foundation Of Dividend Growth

I can think of no better fund to serve as the foundation than the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD). Its forward dividend yield is about 4%, its expense ratio is just 0.06%, and it is broadly diversified across more than 100 blue-chip dividend stocks spanning multiple sectors. SCHD also boasts an impressive dividend-per-share CAGR exceeding 10% over the past decade. However, it lacks exposure to several key sectors, and some retirees may find its 4% yield a bit low. Specifically, it underweights mega-cap technology, utilities (XLU), infrastructure (UTF), and midstream companies (MLPA), and it includes no fixed-income exposure. These four areas will be the focus for the remainder of this portfolio.

JEPQ (or QQQI): High-Yield Tech Exposure

For mega-cap tech exposure, the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ) is an excellent choice for tax-advantaged accounts. It offers juicy monthly distributions that typically yield in the high single digits to low teens annually, depending on market volatility, while providing over 50% exposure to leading mega-cap tech stocks (QQQ). It also features a low 0.35% expense ratio for this type of fund.

If held in a taxable account, however, the Neos Nasdaq 100 High Income ETF (QQQI) may be the better option, despite its higher 0.68% expense ratio. Its yield is similar to JEPQ’s, but its distributions are more tax-efficient thanks to its use of Section 1256 contracts.

Neither of these covered-call funds will be long-term wealth builders, as their structure caps upside potential while leaving them exposed to downside risk. However, as small complementary positions, they can be highly useful for adding diversification into mega-cap tech—especially given their strong total returns relative to SCHD in recent years during the AI-driven tech boom. Their substantial yields also pair nicely with SCHD’s yield, and while they offer little dividend growth, that is not essential here thanks to SCHD’s powerful dividend growth track record.

AMLP: Energy Infrastructure Income

A third fund that can better round out the portfolio is the Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP). It addresses the lack of midstream infrastructure exposure in the portfolio while also adding an attractive combination of yield and growth. Its trailing 12-month dividend yield is about 8.5%, with a dividend growth rate of 6.6% over the past 12 months and 9.7% over the past three years. Going forward, I expect dividend growth to likely remain in the mid-single-digit range, which is a very appealing combination when paired with the 8.5% yield. Additionally, AMLP holds large positions in leading blue-chip midstreams such as Western Midstream Partners (WES), MPLX (MPLX), Energy Transfer (ET), Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), and Plains All-American Pipeline (PAA), giving it a strong underlying portfolio that combines high yield with inflation-beating dividend growth.

UTG: Reliable Utility And Infrastructure Income

A fourth fund to consider is the Reaves Utility Income Trust (UTG), a closed-end fund offering a 5.8% forward dividend yield and a strong history of sustaining or even growing its dividend over time, including a recent 5%+ dividend hike earlier this year. It pays monthly distributions and, with its 5.8% yield, provides a valuable source of steady income. UTG also has significant exposure to leading infrastructure companies, including AI-related names like Vistra Corporation (VST), and it boasts a long-term track record of outperforming the utility sector. Its management team has demonstrated consistent alpha generation as well.

PFFA: Preferred Equity For Reliable Monthly Income

The fifth and final fund that I would include in this portfolio is the Virtus InfraCap U.S. Preferred Stock ETF (PFFA). It is an actively managed preferred equity ETF that has significantly outperformed the broader preferred sector (PFF) over time, paying an attractive 9.4% dividend yield through monthly distributions. While PFFA does use leverage, it applies it prudently in combination with active management to mitigate risk while investing in preferred equities that provide a reliable source of fixed income for investors.

Investor Takeaway

With holdings such as JEPQ, PFFA, and AMLP, investors can achieve a yield of between 8% and 12%, which can then be complemented by UTG’s roughly 6% yield and SCHD’s 4% yield. Regardless of the specific percentage allocation, this combination offers an appealing overall dividend yield, while AMLP, SCHD, and—to a lesser extent—UTG should also provide meaningful dividend growth over time. Provided that JEPQ and PFFA do not make up too large a share of the portfolio, investors should be able to enjoy inflation-beating dividend growth for years to come.

Furthermore, the portfolio will be well diversified across mega-cap tech, midstream infrastructure, utilities and AI-related infrastructure, fixed income, and the broader dividend growth stock universe. Therefore, for investors seeking a relatively simple yet well-diversified portfolio that delivers a powerful mix of current yield and dividend growth, these five funds are excellent choices. Alternatively, investors can save on fund management fees, pursue a bit of greater concentration, and implement value investing principles in pursuit of maximizing yield, dividend growth, and total returns by building their own portfolios of individual stocks and overlaying an opportunistic capital recycling strategy. This is the approach I use to generate a high single digits yield with double-digit annualized dividend growth and total return outperformance in my portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Celsius Holdings: Alani Nu Approaches The Rookie Wall, Beware
data.description:
Celsius Holdings (NASDAQ:CELH) more than doubled year-to-date and nearly tripled from the February lows as investors are increasingly enthusiastic about the Alani Nu acquisition.

With the stock back at a lofty valuation, and as Alani Nu approaches critical levels of market share where many brands have hit a wall, I argue investors should beware and consider trimming their positions.

Introduction To Celsius Holdings

Celsius Holdings took the market by storm after signing a distribution agreement with PepsiCo (PEP) in late 2022, and it's been a volatile ride since. In some moments, Celsius was one of the best-performing stocks in the market, and in others, it was one of the worst.

After all these sharp swings, Celsius shares are up almost double over that period, after shares more than doubled in 2025, driven by the attractive acquisition of Alani Nu, and signs of improvement in the Celsius name brand.

Investors who still remember the burn of the previous drawdown are now asking whether this time it's different, or should they exploit the current enthusiasm before it turns on its head.

Celsius Financials & The PepsiCo Deal

In the second quarter of 2025, Celsius Holdings delivered revenues of $740 million, a gain of $337 million from the prior year quarter, of which $301 million is attributed to the acquisition of Alani Nu.

In other words, the Celsius brand did roughly $438 million in sales, up 9% from last year. Important reminder, last year's second quarter included the first change in PepsiCo's inventory planning, which gives an easier comparison.

The more relevant comparison, in my view, is Celsius end-point sales, which rose by $22 million Y/Y, translating into 130 bps of market share loss.

Below the revenue line, we're seeing Alani Nu's contribution to better margins, as gross margins of 51.5% were about stable, while adjusted EBITDA margins reached a record 28.4%.

All in all, results were quite strong, especially on the profitability front, and that's before the new PepsiCo deal, which we'll get into now.

In what seems to be a win-win arrangement, Celsius is acquiring the Rockstar brand from PepsiCo and plans to move 80% of Alani Nu's distribution to PepsiCo. In exchange, PepsiCo is getting $585 million in convertible preferred stock of Celsius Holdings, increasing its stake to about 11%.

So, a great quarter and a great deal, but there's one issue I'm concerned about.

The Energy Market's Rookie Wall

Let's take a trip down memory lane. It was mid-2024. Celsius stock was at an all-time high of $95 per share, after a strong Q4-23 that beat expectations. It reached an astounding earnings multiple, and the general feeling was that nothing was going to stand in its way from becoming a third giant in the energy drinks market.

At the time, I argued that the stock got ahead of itself and that even a slight hiccup could hurt a lot. In the quarter that followed, the notorious PepsiCo inventory optimization started, sending shares to a near 80% drawdown.

With so much of the focus on the PepsiCo change, many investors missed that the Celsius brand began to lose steam. The numbers that Circana measures, shown in the above graph, represent end-point retail sales, which, by and large, shouldn't have been affected by PepsiCo's moves. As we can see, the Celsius brand turned into a share loser starting in Q3-24, when it reached about a 12% market share.

This is a phenomenon I call the 'Rookie Wall' in the energy drink market. This refers to a phenomenon in sports where a young player gets into a tough stretch after playing in the big leagues for a while.

The history of the energy drink industry tells us that aside from Monster (MNST) and Red Bull, most of the names just come and go. It wasn't so long ago that Logan Paul's Prime was the hottest name, just like the recent acquisition from PepsiCo, Rockstar, had its glory days.

Alani Nu has been crushing it for several years in a row now, but as it reaches a larger scale, the risk of hitting a wall is increasing.

Generally speaking, this is a market of trends, with a lot predicated on social media popularity and choosing the right influencers. Just like it can come quickly, it can go away too.

This is a risk investors have to take into account, but are they?

Valuation & Monster Beverage

At $63 a share, Celsius trades at 61 times forward earnings and 45 times next year's estimates, not yet at the peaks of 2024, but certainly a high mark.

Compared to Monster, Celsius is once again trading at a premium higher than 30%, which we've seen in early 2023, before the sharp widening. Back then, though, Celsius was on track for a year of doubling revenues, while Monster was heading towards 13% growth.

Currently, Monster is expected to deliver a 9% increase in revenues next year, compared to Celsius at 31%. Critically, that 31% is a matter of consolidating Alani Nu. In the relevant quarters that represent the expected underlying growth trend, starting in H2-26, the market is expecting mid-teens growth.

So, we're very far from the fundamental gap that we had back in early 2023. Both the premium over Monster and the valuation in and of itself give me pause. To me, investors are clearly not pricing the risk of an Alani Nu slowdown, probably due to enthusiasm about the PepsiCo agreement.

Conclusion

Celsius Holdings has taken great steps in reigniting the group's trajectory by acquiring Alani Nu and expanding the partnership with PepsiCo.

Investing would have been much easier if stocks simply followed fundamentals, but once again, I believe that Celsius stock has gotten ahead of itself.

This high valuation for a company that doesn't have a wide moat and is vulnerable to unpredictable swings in consumer preferences means significant downside. Meanwhile, with expectations now again very high, the upside is also limited.

As such, I maintain Celsius at "Hold," and I would have rated it a "Sell" if the negative catalysts weren't still a few quarters away.

data.title: TSMC Earnings: I Wish I Hadn't Sold
data.description:
Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited aka TSMC (NYSE:TSM) just released its third quarter earnings. The release beat expectations on revenue and on earnings per share (EPS). Despite the beat, investors reacted poorly to the release, triggering a 1.6% selloff the day after earnings came out.

Going into TSMC's earnings release, I was expecting a pretty good showing. I actually owned TSM stock in the past, but I sold it very early this year after achieving a 2X. I regret having done so, as the stock has since done another 2X, thanks to a string of very good earnings releases.

Despite my earlier sale, I had high expectations for TSM's third quarter release, due to already-released revenue data which showed substantial growth.

Other investors appear to have had high expectations of TSMC as well, as the reaction to the earnings release was actually pretty negative. As mentioned previously, TSM stock declined in value the day after earnings came out. The day was a poor one for markets as a whole, but the decline in TSM's stock price was greater than that in the S&P 500 (SP500) or NASDAQ-100 (NDX), indicating that some catalyst was behind the selloff. Given that the earnings release was the only major news from TSM prior to the start of the trading day, it seems likely that the release triggered the selling.

I disagree with the market's assessment. TSMC appears to be firing on all cylinders following its third quarter earnings release. Revenue is growing, the company is beating expectations, and outlook remains rosy. For these reasons, I remain bullish on TSM stock. However, as TSM has run up so much since I last covered it (with a strong buy rating), I am downgrading my rating to Buy. In the ensuing paragraphs I will explain that decision.

Earnings Recap

TSMC's recent earnings release handily beat investor expectations on all the headline metrics. The results were:

  • Revenue: $33.1 billion, up 41% year over year and 10% sequentially.
  • $2.92 in diluted earnings per share (EPS), up 39% (beat by $0.30).
  • $19.67 billion in gross profit, up 34%.
  • $16.74 billion in operating income, up 38.8%.
  • $17.56 billion in earnings before taxes, up 36.7%.
  • Guidance for revenue between $32.2 billion and $33.4 billion in he next quarter.
  • Guidance for gross profit margin between 55.5% and 57.5%.
  • Guidance for operating margin between 45.5% and 47.5%.

Overall, the headline metrics shown in the release were pretty satisfactory, and ahead of expectations. The forward guidance may have disappointed some investors, as it implied no growth on a quarter over quarter basis. In fact, the lower end of the revenue guidance range even implied negative growth! This fact looks bad on the surface, especially for a stock that is priced for high growth. However, TSM has a very long history of beating earnings expectations, having done so in all of the last four quarters! I would not rule out the possibility of another beat in Q4, along with continued growth.

One possible issue for TSMC is that of declining Chinese market share. This has been an on and off problem for chip companies like Nvidia (NVDA) and Micron (MU). Until now, TSMC has mostly been unaffected, continuing to do business with both Chinese and American chip designers. However, the company is now losing Chinese market share to SMIC (SSE: 688981), which is a major change. Investors will want to continue monitoring this situation, though TSMC itself continues to expect high growth despite its shrinking China market share.

Future Prospects

Having looked at TSMC's most recent earnings release, we can now attempt to gauge its future prospects.

We know that management is guiding for $32.2 billion to $33.4 billion in revenue for the coming quarter. It also expects 59.1% to 61% in gross margin, and 49% to 51% in operating margin. These estimates can lead to a range of possibilities for gross profit and operating profit, which you can see in the table below.

Revenue, low estimate Revenue, high estimate
$32.B 33.4
Gross margin, low estimate $19.03B $19.74B
Gross margin, high estimate $19.64B $20.37B
Operating margin, low estimate $15.78B $16.37B
Operating margin, high estimate $16.42B

$17B

As you can see, there is a wide range of possibilities here, many of them implying higher earnings than what was reported for the third quarter. However, the sequential growth implied by these estimates is definitely lower than what was seen in Q3, even on the high end revenue estimate. For this reason, I expect next quarter's earnings to be $2.92, the same as this quarter. Consistent with this, I expect $9.75 in full year 2025 EPS, a 43% increase compared to the year ago period.

Forecasting TSM's long term earnings growth is necessarily more complex than estimating full year results. The company said that it expected the AI mega-trend to continue, but did not say for how long. I expect some deceleration in both the revenue and earnings growth rates, but I also expect the growth to be positive and fairly high. For the next five years, I could see earnings growing at 20% CAGR, easily. For valuation purposes I will go with 15% CAGR, for the sake of conservatism.

Valuation

Now we can move on to valuing TSM Stock. Its price was $299 as of this writing. This gives a 30.67 P/E ratio for 2025, based on my $2.92 fourth quarter earnings estimate. You can see some more multiples in the image below, using actual data instead of my estimates.

As you can see, the multiples here are about typical for the NASDAQ-100 and above-average for the S&P 500. I believe these indexes are good benchmarks because, while TSMC is a Taiwanese company, it does much business with the U.S. tech sector. It is more similar to Nvidia or Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) than Taiwanese companies in the real estate or banking sectors. So, U.S. tech indexes are better benchmarks for TSM than a Taiwanese market index would be.

Now, based on the benchmark that Seeking Alpha Quant is using (the IT sector), TSM has an "average" valuation. Its P/E and price/book ratios are above average for the IT sector, while its price/cash flow ratio is below average. The multiples earn TSM a D+ value score in Seeking Alpha Quant.

I think TSM is actually a relatively good value today, because it has a lot of growth potential. If earnings grow at 15% CAGR for five years, like I estimated above, then earnings will be $19.61. The estimated P/E ratio after five years, if TSM stock is still at $299, is just 15--below average for a high growth tech stock. So, TSM is not as pricey as it looks.

Risks and Challenges

Despite all the positive things I've written about TSMC in this article, its shareholders nevertheless face risks. These include:

  • Competition in China. About 11% of TSMC's revenue comes from China, whose government is trying to build up SMIC as a viable TSMC alternative. If SMIC gets all Chinese semiconductor manufacturing business, that will be a loss for TSMC.
  • Geopolitical concerns. TSMC is located in Taiwan, as are many of its fabs (semiconductor manufacturing facilities). If China were to invade Taiwan, as some think it will, then TSMC would be at risk. Its fabs could be damaged, as could buildings housing top executives. The economic damage in such a scenario is hard to estimate, but probably pretty large.

My History with TSMC Stock

As a concluding note, I want to share my personal history with TSM stock, as it is a fairly long one and it informs my thesis today.

I first bought TSM stock in late 2022, for a little over $60. I bought the stock shortly after I heard that Warren Buffett and Charlie Munger had bought it for Berkshire Hathaway's (BRK.B) portfolio. I paid a little more than $60 for the stock, continuing to hold it even after Buffett ditched it. I sat on the shares for close to two years, taking what I thought was a "long term view" on the stock. Eventually, though, the stock hit $120, and the urge to take profits overwhelmed me. I sold TSM around that time.

Since then, TSM has done another 2X, and increased its earnings by high percentages. While I made money on TSM, I missed much of the party.

This type of thing often happens with shares of very strong companies. A company like TSMC, that has a competitive edge, high growth rates and high margins, is often worth sitting on forever. In my case, at least, it would have been worth holding on for longer than I did.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Why I Sold Pernod Ricard And Downgrade It To A Hold
data.description:
One of my first articles here on Seeking Alpha focused on Pernod Ricard. Back then, I argued that the sharp share price decline had led to a long-term buying opportunity in a historically stable and profitable industry. But I also cautioned that the company's elevated debt levels could lead to a dividend cut and further volatility.

A year ago, when I wrote the article, Pernod Ricard was less than 1% of my otherwise concentrated value portfolio. The total return over the last year has been -17.45%, but I decided to sell my shares a few weeks after the article was published. Pernod Ricard at the time was a low-conviction bet, and I simply decided to invest the proceeds into a stock that I had higher conviction in.

The share price has further declined since I first covered the company, so let's revisit my thesis. Pernod Ricard has just released new sales data, so we're going to cover this as well.

Pernod Ricard Q1 2026

Pernod Ricard doesn't follow a standard fiscal year, as it starts its year on July 1st. The Q1 sales report just came in, and the numbers didn't impress me.

The company saw overall revenues decline by 14.34% on a reported basis, and fall by 7.6% on an organic basis. Analysts expected a 7.1% organic decline, so the results were slightly worse than expected.

The market, however, reacted positively as the shares have gained more than 6% since the release. Let's dive deeper into the regions.

Americas -12%

Americas (29% of FY25 sales) saw a 12% sales decline. Revenues in the U.S. took a sharp 16% hit. The company stated that sales were impacted by some inventory adjustments, and highlighted that the gap to market continued to close.

According to Nielsen and NACBA numbers, sales at retail in the U.S. were down by roughly 6%. So, the performance wasn't as bad as it seems in its largest market, the U.S., but still the trend is negative.

Asia-RoW -7%

India was the bright spot in the release, as revenues rose by 3%. The country is Pernod Ricard's largest market by volume and second-largest by revenues.

Sales in India were impacted by the 50% increase in excise taxes in the state of Maharashtra implemented in July. The state represents 8.84% of India's population, and 14% of the company's Indian sales. The excise increase led to a ~35% increase in retail prices, which of course impacts demand.

If we exclude the impact of the excise taxes, sales in India would have risen by roughly 7%.

For the full year, the company expects the excise increase to continue to weigh on the overall sales performance in its second-largest market by value.

India is booming, there's no question about it. But over the years, I've learned that emerging markets tend to be volatile and cyclical. It's therefore encouraging that Pernod Ricard also has a strong presence in China. It has been a drag on performance in recent years, but over the long term, I'm quite bullish on the country.

In Q1, however, sales were once again disappointing, as China saw a staggering 27% revenue decline. Sales were impacted by the still challenging macro environment, trade inventory adjustments, and the tightened regulatory environment, which affects cognac sales in particular.

China's eight-day Mid-Autumn festival is almost 3 weeks later this year, and therefore will be reflected in the next quarter's sales numbers instead of Q1.

The festival's sales numbers were a drag on the recent data as they weren't included, but we have further data on this event. It seems like this year's alcohol sales fell behind last year's by a wide margin.

Management stated that the underlying performance of Q1 in China was better than the numbers reflect, and while full visibility is difficult, the factors above probably had a significant impact.

I agree, but nonetheless, China's appetite for alcohol is in a serious downtrend. In 2024, Pernod Ricard saw a 10% sales decline in the country, followed by a 21% drop in 2025, and now a further 27% hit in Q1 2026.

If I had to find a positive in the China figures, it's that premium brands like Jameson continued to grow in Q1.

Other than that, Pernod Ricard highlighted strong growth in Japan, as well as strong results in Africa and the Middle East.

Overall, China overshadowed the strong performance in India and other countries, as Asia-RoW, the largest region by sales (42% in FY25), saw revenues decline by 7%.

Europe & Global Travel Retail

Europe (29% of sales in FY25) and travel retail (6% of sales) saw revenues decline by 4% and 15% respectively.

In Spain and Germany, the rate of decline eased. And France and the U.K. saw a modest decline. If there's one constant in the Q1 numbers, it's that sales were lower than last year.

Global travel retail was especially weak, but it was impacted by the suspension of cognac sales in China. Sales are expected to resume in Q2. Management expects the travel retail segment to return to growth this fiscal year.

Guidance for FY26 and FY27-29

As we all know, markets tend to be forward-looking. The Q1 numbers were slightly worse than analysts expected, but the shares nevertheless started to rally after the release. Management's positive outlook certainly didn't hurt.

The company is trying to defend the operating margin during the current downturn by implementing an operational efficiency program. It also expects an improving trend in organic sales in H2 of this fiscal year. There's a focus on cash generation, and the cash conversion is expected to come in stronger this year.

Over the medium term, the future could be bright, according to the company. It expects organic sales growth to turn positive beginning from FY27 and is aiming for 3% to 6% annual growth. The organic operating margin is expected to expand, supported by cost-cutting measures and reduced CapEx.

Pernod Ricard Has to Deliver

Since my first article last year, the industry has faced continued pressures. Also, in my initial analysis, I made the mistake of relying on the provided EPS numbers instead of focusing on FCF.

In 2022, Pernod Ricard generated almost €1.8 billion in FCF. Just two years later, FCF has dropped to €954 million, but has since recovered to €1.12 billion in the last fiscal year.

The company pays €1.2 billion in annual dividends, which is slightly above its current FCF generation. Over the long term, this is not sustainable, unless FCF generation recovers significantly from here.

Net debt is above €10 billion. That's significant for a company that generated just EUR1.1 billion in FCF last year.

The current dividend yield is 5.27%. Pernod Ricard has to return to growth, or the current dividend distributions won't be sustainable.

A dividend cut would further add to volatility, as the relatively high dividend yield is attractive to income-oriented investors, who would likely sell out if the distributions were cut. That would be the right move given the challenges and debt levels, but nonetheless, would likely be punished by the market.

The company's market cap is €21.82 billion, for a P/FCF of 19.48. If we assume FCF recovers to pre-pandemic levels (~€1.35 billion annual FCF), the P/FCF would still be 16.16x.

Even though shares have declined by 60% over the last few years, that's still elevated for a company facing a severe downturn with a significant debt load and a dividend that exceeds its FCF.

It's fair to say that Pernod Ricard was vastly overvalued before the industry started its decline. Even at current levels, the shares don't offer a margin of safety, if we calculate its intrinsic value by its FCF generation.

I sold all my shares and don't have skin in the game. Therefore, I can't justify a "Buy" rating any longer, and downgrade the shares to a "Hold".

That said, the main reason for my sellout was further concentration of my portfolio, by selling down low-conviction bets to increase my positions where I have higher conviction.

Pernod Ricard holds brands that date back centuries. There aren't many consumer products that are still in high demand generation after generation. Alcohol consumption is facing a severe downturn, but I believe it'll recover.

If Pernod Ricard has to cut its dividend and shares were to decline further, I'll revisit my thesis, and it could become a high-conviction long-term play. It's all a matter of valuation.

Usually the best opportunities arise in times of crisis, but investors in the spirits industry may need more patience. There's nothing wrong with starting to dollar-cost average at current levels, and most importantly, keep contributing if shares were to decline further. Over the long term, I remain bullish on Pernod Ricard as well as the industry.

Conclusion

Pernod Ricard's business is still backed by strong brands, and I believe the long-term case for spirits remains intact.

But in my initial analysis, I placed too much emphasis on EPS rather than focusing on FCF generation. Given the cash generation, debt levels, and ongoing industry headwinds, the shares no longer appear attractively valued even though price declined significantly over the last few years.

I've stepped to the sidelines not because I think the company is doomed over the long term, but because there are simply better places for my capital at this point.

The Q1 numbers show continued pressure and could put the dividend at risk, if the environment doesn't improve from here. I'll be ready to take a further look if and when that happens, but for now I can't justify a "Buy" rating, especially since I don't have skin in the game myself.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
